articles.title: Registration storm RCA on the P-CSCF edge: retry amplification before downstream collapse
articles.summary: Use this article when REGISTER traffic spikes faster than unique subscribers, retransmissions dominate new work, and the registration path degrades at the P-CSCF edge before S-CSCF or HSS show a full outage.

primary_signals: REGISTER transactions rise sharply while unique subscriber count grows much more slowly.
Retransmission count accelerates with the REGISTER spike, which means the edge is reprocessing the same registration attempts instead of only handling new work.
P-CSCF queue depth, worker saturation, or transaction-table pressure rise before S-CSCF and HSS show comparable failure severity.
supporting_signals: Repeated Call-ID, Contact, or IMPU values appear across a short time window.
401 or 423 responses remain common for too long before the flow converts to 200 OK.
Latency expands on the registration path quickly enough to trigger additional UE retries.
signals_that_do_not_fit: Broad 5xx failures from S-CSCF or HSS across multiple call types usually indicate downstream service failure, not a pure registration storm.
Healthy retransmission levels with high REGISTER volume more often indicate a planned burst or provisioning event than retry amplification.

differential_diagnosis.condition: Synthetic load burst or bad traffic profile
what_to_check: Compare REGISTER TPS with the active SIPp or generator rate profile.
Check whether the same source range or same scenario revision started immediately before the spike.
differential_diagnosis.why_it_matters: In the demo and many lab environments, the fastest path to recovery is reducing the injector rate, not treating the event as a core-network outage.

differential_diagnosis.condition: Lost authentication challenge state across edge replicas
what_to_check: Look for repeated 401 loops on the same subscriber without a stable 200 conversion.
Check whether traffic is bouncing between replicas without preserving challenge continuity.
differential_diagnosis.why_it_matters: Challenge-state loss turns one registration into several incomplete attempts and can create a storm with normal downstream capacity.

differential_diagnosis.condition: Short registration expiry or bad refresh margin
what_to_check: Review the configured expiry returned to subscribers and the observed refresh spacing.
Confirm whether registrations succeed but renew far earlier than expected.
differential_diagnosis.why_it_matters: A policy defect can sustain high REGISTER load even when the network and platform are otherwise healthy.

differential_diagnosis.condition: Downstream S-CSCF saturation masquerading as edge overload
what_to_check: Compare P-CSCF ingress timing with S-CSCF processing latency and backlog.
Check whether call setup or mid-session transactions are also degrading, not just registration.
differential_diagnosis.why_it_matters: If the downstream tier is the real bottleneck, scaling only the edge will lengthen the incident and hide the actual failure domain.

evidence_to_collect.signal: REGISTER TPS versus unique subscriber count
evidence_to_collect.expected_if_true: Transaction volume rises much faster than unique users, proving the incident is dominated by retries or premature refresh rather than organic subscriber growth.

evidence_to_collect.signal: Retransmission count and repeated Call-ID patterns
evidence_to_collect.expected_if_true: The same registration attempts recur frequently within one short interval, which is the strongest sign of retry amplification.

evidence_to_collect.signal: P-CSCF shard or pod hot spotting
evidence_to_collect.expected_if_true: One ingress shard, pod, or load-balancer target runs materially hotter than its peers while registration latency clusters on that path.
Challenge or interval responses dominate longer than normal and successful completion lags until the retry source is damped.

evidence_to_collect.signal: S-CSCF and HSS health relative to edge pressure
evidence_to_collect.expected_if_true: Downstream components remain comparatively stable while edge transaction pressure and latency rise first.

recommended_rca.root_cause: Retry amplification on the registration path is saturating the P-CSCF edge before downstream IMS tiers fully fail, usually because synthetic pacing, premature refresh behavior, or delayed challenge completion causes the same subscribers to reattempt registration too quickly.
recommended_rca.explanation: A real registration storm is usually not just high REGISTER volume. The more reliable pattern is REGISTER transactions increasing faster than unique subscribers while retransmissions, repeated Call-ID values, and delayed challenge-to-success conversion pile up on the P-CSCF edge. When that happens, the edge consumes capacity reprocessing the same work, latency stretches enough to trigger more retries, and the incident looks severe even though S-CSCF or HSS may still be comparatively healthy. If shard-level pressure and retry evidence align, the RCA should describe edge retry amplification first and treat downstream collapse as a later or secondary risk.
recommended_rca.recommendation: Confirm whether the surge is synthetic or policy-driven, reduce the retry source before making broad platform changes, then add P-CSCF capacity only if legitimate demand still exceeds the edge envelope. Capture shard-level edge evidence and challenge-conversion ratios before escalating to S-CSCF or HSS owners.

operator_actions.action: Compare REGISTER TPS with unique IMPU or IMSI count for the same incident window.
operator_actions.expected_signal: If retries dominate, transaction volume grows much faster than the subscriber population.

operator_actions.action: Sample repeating Call-ID, Contact, or subscriber identifiers from the hottest window.
operator_actions.expected_signal: Repeated identity patterns confirm the storm is recycled work, not only new registrations.

operator_actions.action: Check whether one P-CSCF shard or ingress target is materially hotter than the rest.
operator_actions.expected_signal: Localized overload indicates load-distribution or sticky-state problems before a global capacity shortfall.

operator_actions.action: Review 401, 423, timeout, and 200 conversion timing on the registration path.
operator_actions.expected_signal: A long challenge loop or delayed completion points to retry amplification rather than a simple surge of legitimate successes.

operator_actions.action: Compare edge distress with S-CSCF and HSS health before escalating.
operator_actions.expected_signal: If downstream health is still mostly stable, keep the RCA focused on edge retry behavior and pacing.

safe_actions: Throttle or stagger the traffic generator if the burst is synthetic.
Scale the P-CSCF edge only after confirming the issue is not purely caused by bad pacing or lost challenge state.
Preserve a few repeated subscriber or Call-ID examples in the incident note so future RCA can reference exact evidence.
escalation_signals: S-CSCF latency, timeout rate, or worker saturation rises in lockstep with the REGISTER storm.
HSS vector or auth failures affect multiple unrelated subscriber cohorts after edge pressure is reduced.
The incident persists after the generator rate is lowered and challenge-state continuity is verified.
pitfalls: Do not call it a generic capacity incident if the dominant symptom is the same subscribers retrying registration.
Do not scale blindly before checking whether one ingress shard is hot or whether the traffic is synthetic.
Do not blame HSS first when the strongest evidence still points to P-CSCF queueing and retry loops.
telemetry_queries: register_rate over 1m compared with unique subscriber count over the same 1m window
top repeating Call-ID or IMPU values during the hottest registration interval
P-CSCF per-pod request rate, queue depth, and latency distribution
401 or 423 to 200 conversion ratio on the registration path

timeline: Synthetic registration burst begins and REGISTER TPS climbs sharply.
P-CSCF queue depth rises first, followed by higher retransmission count and repeated identities.
Challenge completion falls behind and subscribers reattempt registration before previous attempts settle.
Only after sustained edge pressure do downstream symptoms begin to appear.
reference_incident_pattern.why_this_pattern_is_realistic: This sequence matches how registration storms commonly present in real IMS operations: retry feedback and edge admission pressure are visible before the rest of the control plane fully degrades.
